request.question: When did Limp Bizkit become a rap rock band?

title: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all
Limp Bizkit
Icon (Limp Bizkit album)
Eat You Alive
Ready to Go (Limp Bizkit song)
Results May Vary
Limp Bizkit discography
Significant Other
Stampede of the Disco Elephants
Home Sweet Home/Bittersweet Symphony
sentences: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all$ is the debut studio album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.

Released on July 1, 1997, the album established the band's trademark sound with the singles "Counterfeit", which was influenced by hip hop and heavy metal, and "Faith", a cover of a song by George Michael.
Limp Bizkit's rearrangement of the song incorporated heavier guitar playing by Wes Borland and scratching by DJ Lethal.
Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994.
Their lineup consists of Fred Durst (lead vocals), Sam Rivers (bass, backing vocals), John Otto (drums, percussion), and Wes Borland (guitars, backing vocals).
Their music is marked by Durst's angry vocal delivery and Borland's sonic experimentation.
Borland's elaborate visual appearance, which includes face and body paint, masks and uniforms, also plays a large role in the band's elaborate live shows.
The band has been nominated for three Grammy Awards, have sold 40 million records worldwide and won several other awards.
Icon is the fourth compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.

Released in 2011, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".
"Eat You Alive" is a song by the band Limp Bizkit.
It was released in September 2003 as a single from their fourth studio album "Results May Vary" (2003).
The song was written by Fred Durst, John Otto, Sam Rivers and Mike Smith, and is Limp Bizkit's first single without Wes Borland, who had left the band in 2001.
"Ready to Go" is a song by the American nu metal band Limp Bizkit.
The single features rapper and then label-mate Lil Wayne and is produced by Polow Da Don.
The single is Limp Bizkit's first release for Cash Money Records after their departure from Interscope in 2011.
The song was released in March 2013 as a free download on the band's website and, on April 16, as a digital single on iTunes and Amazon.
Results May Vary is the fourth studio album by the American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.
Released on September 23, 2003, it is the band's only release under the sole-leadership of vocalist Fred Durst after the temporary departure of guitarist Wes Borland, who left the band in 2001.

Snot guitarist Mike Smith was initially brought in to replace Borland, although the band's falling-out with Smith later lead to his departure, with much of the material recorded with him being discarded from the final release.
Durst and a number of guests ended up handling the majority of the album's guitar work.

The discography of Limp Bizkit, an American nu metal band, consists of five studio albums, three compilation albums, one remix album, one live album, one extended play, 26 singles, three promotional singles, 27 music videos and two video albums.
Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida.
The band has sold an estimated 50 million albums worldwide.
Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.
Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.
"Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.
The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.
Stampede of the Disco Elephants is the upcoming sixth studio album by American nu metal band Limp Bizkit.
It is set to be Limp Bizkit's first album to be released since DJ Lethal's departure in May 2012.
After the original line-up reunited in 2009, "Gold Cobra" was released in 2011.
The band left Interscope and signed up with Cash Money Records in February 2012.
The first single "Lightz" was released on October 26, 2012.
After many delays, the second single for the album, "Ready to Go", featuring label mate Lil Wayne was released on April 16, 2013, to positive reviews.
It is the first album since "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)" to be produced by Ross Robinson.
"Home Sweet Home / Bittersweet Symphony", also known as "Bittersweet Home" is a song by American rock group Limp Bizkit.
It was released in December 2005 as the only single from the compilation album "Greatest Hitz".
The song is a medley of "Home Sweet Home" by Mötley Crüe and "Bitter Sweet Symphony" by The Verve.

It is, along with the songs "Why" and "Lean On Me", the only new content on the "Greatest Hitz" compilation, and is the third cover song Limp Bizkit has released on an album (the others being The Who's "Behind Blue Eyes", and George Michael's "Faith").

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

Released on July 1, 1997, the album established the band's trademark sound with the singles "Counterfeit", which was influenced by hip hop and heavy metal, and "Faith", a cover of a song by George Michael.  Limp Bizkit's rearrangement of the song incorporated heavier guitar playing by Wes Borland and scratching by DJ Lethal.

Significant Other | Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.  "Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.  The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.

Limp Bizkit | Limp Bizkit is an American rap rock band from Jacksonville, Florida, formed in 1994.  Their lineup consists of Fred Durst (lead vocals), Sam Rivers (bass, backing vocals), John Otto (drums, percussion), and Wes Borland (guitars, backing vocals).  Their music is marked by Durst's angry vocal delivery and Borland's sonic experimentation.  Borland's elaborate visual appearance, which includes face and body paint, masks and uniforms, also plays a large role in the band's elaborate live shows.  The band has been nominated for three Grammy Awards, have sold 40 million records worldwide and won several other awards.

Greatest Hitz (Limp Bizkit album) | Greatest Hitz is the second compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 2005, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water" and "Results May Vary".

Icon (Limp Bizkit album) | Icon is the fourth compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 2011, it is a retrospective compiling material from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".

Collected (Limp Bizkit album) | Collected is the third compilation album by American rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 2008, it a retrospective compilation album from the band's albums "Three Dollar Bill, Yall$", "Significant Other", "Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water", "Results May Vary" and "The Unquestionable Truth (Part 1)".

Limp Bizkit discography | The discography of Limp Bizkit, an American nu metal band, consists of five studio albums, three compilation albums, one remix album, one live album, one extended play, 26 singles, three promotional singles, 27 music videos and two video albums.  Limp Bizkit formed in 1995 in Jacksonville, Florida.  The band has sold an estimated 50 million albums worldwide.
response.summary_1: They became a rap rock band when they formed in 1994 (their 1997 debut album then established their trademark rap‑rock sound).
response.hop2_query: They became a rap-rock band when they formed in 1994; their 1997 debut album, Three Dollar Bill, Y'all$, then established their trademark rap-rock sound.
hop2_docs: Three Dollar Bill, Y'all | Three Dollar Bill, Y'all$ is the debut studio album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released on July 1, 1997, the album established the band's trademark sound with the singles "Counterfeit", which was influenced by hip hop and heavy metal, and "Faith", a cover of a song by George Michael.  Limp Bizkit's rearrangement of the song incorporated heavier guitar playing by Wes Borland and scratching by DJ Lethal.

Significant Other | Significant Other is the second album by American rap rock band Limp Bizkit.  Released in 1999 by Flip/Interscope Records, the album saw the band expanding its sound from that of its debut album "Three Dollar Bill, Yall", to incorporate further metal and hip hop influences.  "Significant Other" was co-produced by Terry Date and Limp Bizkit.  The album has sold at least 16 million copies worldwide.

List of rap rock bands | The following is a list of rap rock bands "with articles" on Wikipedia.  The list includes bands that have been described as a form of rap rock, including the subgenres rapcore and rap metal, by professional journalists at some stage in their career.

The Art of Breaking | The Art of Breaking is the third studio album by Christian rock band Thousand Foot Krutch that was released by Tooth & Nail Records in July 2005.  The band diverges even farther from the rap-rock from their past albums, omitting rap segments almost entirely in favor of a more mainstream rock sound or traditional heavy metal elements.  The single "Move" received moderate mainstream success, reaching No. 16 on "Billboard"' s mainstream rock charts in early 2006.  The album has three singles: "Move", "Absolute" and "Breathe You In".  Of these, only "Move" received a music video.  The band has said that the girl on the album's cover is the girl in the video for "Move", as shown in a picture on their MySpace.

Getting Away with Murder (album) | Getting Away with Murder is the third album by the rock band Papa Roach.  The album was a departure from the band's previous works, showcasing an alternative rock and hard rock sound instead of a nu metal and rap metal sound.  Unlike their previous albums, the album features no rapping and instead features Jacoby Shaddix singing melodies.  It was released on August 31, 2004 via Geffen Records and has been certified Gold in Canada and Platinum in America, selling at least 2.8 million units.  "Getting Away with Murder" peaked at number 17 on the "Billboard" 200.

Hed PE | Hed PE, also known as (hed) Planet Earth and stylized as (həd) p.e., is an American rap rock band from Huntington Beach, California.  Formed in 1994, the band performs a style of music which is referred to as "G-punk", a fusion of punk rock and gangsta rap.

Bad Ronald (band) | Bad Ronald was a rap-rock band from New York City who released their self-titled debut album in 2001.  Their first single, "Let's Begin", was briefly on MTV's rotation and featured in the film "Not Another Teen Movie".  Their second single, "1st Time", was featured in the 2002 movie "Orange County".  The band achieved little success in spite of their promotion with MTV.  The group formed in 1999 after working shows in New York City.  The group wanted "to form a band that combined rap, pop, rock, and sophomoric humor."
response.summary_2: They became a rap rock band when they formed in 1994; their 1997 debut album established their trademark rap‑rock sound.
